evals.prompt: Use Fan Solo to deeply research whether passkeys are replacing passwords in consumer finance. Use Parallel and Perplexity where available, cross-check the evidence, and give me a cited report.
evals.expected_output: Frame a dated research brief, create distinct primary-source, landscape, counterevidence, and independent-synthesis lanes, use Parallel CLI as the preferred primary artifact path, Perplexity as an independent challenge lane, Parallel Search MCP as fallback, and Parallel Task MCP only for a warranted final deep-verification pass, then reconcile underlying sources rather than provider votes.
assertions: Routes deep multi-provider research to solo-deep-research
Creates distinct evidence lanes instead of duplicate provider prompts
Prefers Parallel CLI, then Perplexity, with Parallel MCP used as fallback or final verification
Uses Solo scratchpad, todos, recorded child IDs, and timers for asynchronous work
Treats repeated URLs across providers as one source
Requires citations and reports unresolved disagreement

evals.prompt: Use Solo to find the current stable Rust version.
evals.expected_output: Treat this as a narrow lookup, use one current search source or official Rust source, cite it, and avoid multi-agent fan-out or paid deep-research tasks.
assertions: Does not fan out a simple fact lookup
Prefers an authoritative current source
Avoids paid or asynchronous deep-research tooling

evals.prompt: Deeply compare three database vendors. Parallel Task MCP is unavailable and Perplexity is rate-limited, but parallel-cli works.
evals.expected_output: Record unavailable providers, reroute independent lanes through distinct parallel-cli search, extract, and research questions, save artifacts, preserve source independence, and avoid installing tools, authenticating, or purchasing credits.
assertions: Falls back to available parallel-cli without blocking
Keeps lanes distinct despite using one provider
Saves JSON or Markdown artifacts and records run IDs
Does not install, authenticate, or add credit without authority

evals.prompt: Research this before we plan it: replace Redis with Kafka because our notifications are occasionally delayed.
evals.expected_output: Clarify only material ambiguity, separate delayed notifications from the proposed Redis-to-Kafka migration, frame concrete questions about the current code path, load, failure evidence, and delivery requirements, inspect code and official dependency docs, evaluate Kafka against simpler root-cause fixes, and stress-test the recommendation with specific operational and user-facing risks.
assertions: Researches the notification delay rather than anchoring on Kafka
Inspects local code, configuration, callers, tests, and dependency versions
Evaluates the proposed migration explicitly against simpler alternatives
Names concrete downsides and failure modes
Hands verified context to planning instead of implementing

evals.prompt: Deeply research how we should improve onboarding.
evals.expected_output: Recognize that audience, target outcome, and in-scope onboarding surface materially change the research; batch concise tailored choices with plain-language tradeoffs before dispatch, then use only relevant codebase, user-impact, docs, and external-pattern lanes.
assertions: Asks only material questions specific to onboarding
Explains option tradeoffs in plain language
Does not launch a generic maximum-size cohort
Includes user-flow, accessibility, error-state, and codebase evidence when relevant

evals.prompt: Can you dig really deep into whether WebAuthn adoption is actually overtaking SMS one-time codes right now? I want trustworthy sources, not vibes.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-deep-research to frame a research brief, fan out independent primary-source, landscape, and counterevidence lanes across available providers, reconcile underlying sources rather than provider votes, and return a cited synthesis that flags any unresolved disagreement.
assertions: Reads the casual phrasing as a deep, current, cross-checked research request
Creates independent evidence lanes rather than a single query
Requires citations and surfaces unresolved disagreement

evals.prompt: $solo-deep-research: exhaustively compare Turso, Neon, and PlanetScale for a serverless Postgres workload, with cited tradeoffs and any conflicting benchmarks reconciled.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-deep-research to build distinct per-vendor and cross-cutting lanes, prefer Parallel CLI with Perplexity as an independent challenge lane, treat repeated sources as one, and deliver a cited comparison that resolves or names conflicting benchmarks.
assertions: Honors the explicit skill invocation for comparative research
Keeps vendor lanes independent instead of duplicate prompts
Reconciles conflicting benchmarks and cites sources

evals.prompt: Deeply research the current state of Rust async runtimes with citations, then drop the recommendation into a scratchpad the team can pick up.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-deep-research to run the cross-checked, cited research and synthesis, and land the durable handoff in a scratchpad as part of the research deliverable rather than treating the write-up as separate work.
assertions: Routes the deep cited research to solo-deep-research
Treats the scratchpad handoff as the research deliverable, not a separate skill hop
Keeps citations and a durable handoff

evals.prompt: Before we commit to moving billing to event sourcing, research whether it actually fixes our reconciliation bugs. Cross-check the claims and stress-test the recommendation.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-deep-research to separate the reconciliation symptom from the proposed event-sourcing migration, inspect local code and official dependency docs, evaluate the proposal against simpler root-cause fixes, and stress-test the recommendation with concrete operational risks before handing verified context to planning.
assertions: Researches the reconciliation problem rather than anchoring on event sourcing
Evaluates the proposal against simpler alternatives
Names concrete downsides and hands context to planning instead of implementing

evals.prompt: Give me an exhaustive, cross-checked rundown of how the OAuth 2.1 draft differs from 2.0 and where the ecosystem consensus actually sits, with citations and any disagreements called out.
evals.expected_output: Use $solo-deep-research to fan out primary-spec, ecosystem-landscape, and counterevidence lanes, cite each claim, and report consensus alongside any unresolved disagreement rather than flattening it.
assertions: Treats the comparative, current-standard request as deep research
Uses distinct primary-source and landscape lanes
Cites claims and preserves unresolved disagreement

evals.prompt: Break this large migration into independent implementation lanes and run several agents in parallel to build them out.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; route to $solo-orchestrate-agents for parallel disjoint-lane implementation with ownership, locks, and evidence review, since the deliverable is built code rather than a cited research synthesis.
assertions: Routes parallel implementation to solo-orchestrate-agents
Does not treat build work as cited research

evals.prompt: Run a cross-model review of this diff across a couple of agents and reconcile their findings into one verdict.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; route to $solo-orchestrate-agents for cross-model review with dispatch, reconciliation, and evidence review, since the deliverable is a review verdict rather than externally cited research.
assertions: Routes cross-model code review to solo-orchestrate-agents
Distinguishes reconciling reviewers from reconciling cited sources

evals.prompt: What port does Postgres listen on by default?
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; this is a narrow lookup the lead resolves with its own single authoritative check, no skill and no multi-agent fan-out.
assertions: Treats a single fact as a narrow lookup
Uses no skill and no fan-out

evals.prompt: Check the newest published version of the @stellar/stellar-sdk package.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; the lead answers this with its own single registry or search check, no skill and no paid deep-research tooling.
assertions: Keeps a version lookup with the lead's own search
Avoids paid or asynchronous deep-research tooling

evals.prompt: Pull the exact signature of soroban-sdk's storage API straight from the official docs.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; the lead reads the one authoritative doc directly, no skill and no research fan-out.
assertions: Treats a single doc read as a narrow lookup
Does not fan out lanes for one authoritative source

evals.prompt: The research is already done -- just write our findings up into a clean scratchpad for the next agent to pick up.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; route to $solo-keep-scratchpads to persist already-gathered findings, since no new evidence gathering or synthesis is requested.
assertions: Routes persisting finished findings to solo-keep-scratchpads
Does not launch new research when synthesis is already complete

evals.prompt: Configure and launch a single Solo agent to check whether we import lodash anywhere in the repo.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; route to $solo-work-with-agents for the single-agent session, since this is one bounded codebase check rather than multi-lane cited research.
assertions: Routes a single-agent codebase check to solo-work-with-agents
Does not fan out research lanes for one bounded question

evals.prompt: I'm new to Solo and not sure which capability fits -- help me pick the right skill to coordinate a big multi-part task.
evals.expected_output: Do not use $solo-deep-research; route to $fan-solo to choose among Solo skills, since the request is capability routing rather than research synthesis.
assertions: Routes Solo capability selection to fan-solo
Does not treat skill routing as a research task
